differences.description: The document title changed from “The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual: Volume II” to “The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual, Volume II.”
The document version and release status changed from “Version 20250508: This document is in Ratified state.” to “Version 20260120: Official Release.”
The contributors paragraph no longer has a double period after “Reinoud Zandijk”; it now ends with a single period.
The footer/manual title changed from “The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual: Volume II” to “The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual, Volume II.”
A new current-release preface for Version 20260120 was added, stating that the document contains listed ISA modules all of which have been ratified.
The module list for the current release now includes “Svrsw60t59b Extension” version 1.0.

The current release module table was changed from a three-column Module/Version/Status table with every status shown as Ratified to a two-column Module/Version table preceded by text stating all modules are ratified.
The module name “Pointer Masking” was changed to “Pointer-Masking Extensions.”
The 20250508 module list was converted from the current preface in the first document to a historical “Preface to Version 20250508” in the second document.
Several module names in the 20250508 table were normalized to include “Extension,” including “Smctr Extension,” “Svvptc Extension,” and “Ssqosid Extension.”
The bullet “Addition of the Ssqosid Extension for Quality-of-Service Identifiers” now ends with a period.
The bullet “Addition of the Pointer Masking Extension” was changed to “Addition of the Pointer-Masking Extensions.”
A change log entry was added for changes since version 20250508: “Addition of the Svrsw60t59b extension for additional PTE reserved-for-software bits.”
The compatible-change bullet about synchronization requirements was expanded from “changing the PBMTE fields in menvcfg and henvcfg” to “changing the PBMTE and ADUE fields in menvcfg and henvcfg.”
A compatible-change bullet was added: “Defined the format of the memory-mapped msip registers.”
The compatible-change bullet for mseccfg was changed from “Defined optional mseccfg and mseccfgh CSRs” to “Defined mseccfg and mseccfgh CSRs,” removing the statement that they are optional.
The debug specification reference changed from “The separate debug specification proposal describes operation...” to “The separate debug specification describes operation...,” removing “proposal.”
The description of csr[9:8] was expanded to state that the pattern 10 represents hypervisor CSRs.

The CSR listing introduction changed from listing Table 4–Table 8 to listing Table 4–Table 7, because the second document reorganizes machine-level CSR tables and adds a separate indirect CSR mapping subsection.

differences.section: 2.2.1. Currently allocated RISC-V unprivileged CSR addresses
differences.description: The unprivileged CSR table was placed under a new subsection heading “2.2.1. Currently allocated RISC-V unprivileged CSR addresses.”

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The supervisor-level CSR table was placed under a new subsection heading “2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses.”

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Supervisor Indirect” CSR group was added to the supervisor-level CSR listing, containing siselect at 0x150 and sireg, sireg2, sireg3, sireg4, sireg5, and sireg6 at 0x151, 0x152, 0x153, 0x155, 0x156, and 0x157.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Supervisor Timer Compare” CSR group was added, listing stimecmp at 0x14D and stimecmph at 0x15D.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Supervisor Resource Management Configuration” CSR group was added, listing srmcfg at 0x181.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Supervisor Control Transfer Records Configuration” CSR group was added, listing sctrctl at 0x14E, sctrstatus at 0x14F, and sctrdepth at 0x15F.

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: The hypervisor and VS CSR table was placed under a new subsection heading “2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses.”

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Virtual Supervisor Indirect” CSR group was added, listing vsiselect at 0x250 and vsireg, vsireg2, vsireg3, vsireg4, vsireg5, and vsireg6 at 0x251, 0x252, 0x253, 0x255, 0x256, and 0x257.

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Virtual Supervisor Timer Compare” CSR group was added, listing vstimecmp at 0x24D and vstimecmph at 0x25D.

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Virtual Supervisor Control Transfer Records Configuration” CSR group was added, listing vsctrctl at 0x24E.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The machine-level CSR listing was placed under a new subsection heading “2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses.”

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Machine Indirect” CSR group was added, listing miselect at 0x350 and mireg, mireg2, mireg3, mireg4, mireg5, and mireg6 at 0x351, 0x352, 0x353, 0x355, 0x356, and 0x357.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The two machine-level CSR tables from the first document were consolidated into a single Table 7 in the second document.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The machine counter setup CSR listing now includes mcyclecfg at 0x321 and minstretcfg at 0x322.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The machine counter setup CSR listing now includes mcyclecfgh at 0x721 and minstretcfgh at 0x722.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: A “Machine Control Transfer Records Configuration” CSR group was added, listing mctrctl at 0x34E.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind) mappings
differences.description: A new subsection was added listing currently allocated indirect CSR mappings for M-mode.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind) mappings
differences.description: The new M-mode indirect CSR mapping table adds miselect mappings 0x30–0x3F for iprio0 through iprio15.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind) mappings
differences.description: The new M-mode indirect CSR mapping table adds miselect mappings 0x70 for eidelivery, 0x72 for eithreshold, 0x80–0xBF for eip0 through eip63, and 0xC0–0xFF for eie0 through eie63, with specified zero or none entries for other alias registers.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind/Sscsrind) mappings
differences.description: A new S-mode indirect CSR mapping table was added.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind/Sscsrind) mappings
differences.description: The new S-mode indirect CSR mapping table adds siselect mappings 0x40, 0x42, and 0x43–0x5F for delegated cycle, instret, hpmcounter, cyclecfg, instretcfg, hpmevent, and high-half counter/configuration state.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind/Sscsrind) mappings
differences.description: The new S-mode indirect CSR mapping table adds siselect mappings 0x200–0x2FF for CTR entries ctrsource0–ctrsource255, ctrtarget0–ctrtarget255, and ctrdata0–ctrdata255.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind/Sscsrind) mappings
differences.description: A new VS-mode indirect CSR mapping table was added, including AIA-related mappings and CTR mappings 0x200–0x2FF.
The commentary describing architecture IDs changed “the repo around which development occurs” to “the project around which development occurs.”
The mhartid requirement changed from “at least one hart must have a hart ID of zero” to “one hart must have a hart ID of zero.”

differences.section: 3.1.6.2. Double Trap Control in mstatus Register
differences.description: The critical-error-state description now states that whether performance counters and timers are updated in the critical-error state is UNSPECIFIED.
The MPRV description was broadened from “loads and stores” to “explicit memory accesses” when describing what executes with modified effective privilege.
The TW description now refers to WFI execution “in modes less privileged than M” rather than “in lower privilege modes” or “less-privileged modes.”

The U-mode WFI rule was changed to state that when S-mode is implemented, executing WFI in U-mode causes an illegal-instruction exception regardless of TW unless it completes within the bounded time limit.
The statement that a future revision might add a feature allowing S-mode to selectively permit WFI in U-mode was removed.

Machine software interrupt delivery was changed from being described as written by memory-mapped control registers “used by remote harts” and by a hart writing its own MSIP bit, to being written by memory-mapped control registers used to provide machine-level interprocessor interrupts.
A hart’s memory-mapped msip register is now defined as a 32-bit read/write register with bits 31–1 reading as zero and bit 0 containing MSIP.
The second document specifies that changes to the memory-mapped msip register are guaranteed to be reflected in mip.MSIP eventually, but not necessarily immediately.

The supervisor timer interrupt description was updated to distinguish behavior when stimecmp is not implemented versus when stimecmp is implemented; with stimecmp implemented, STIP is read-only and reflects the stimecmp timer interrupt signal.
The RV32 CSR access description contains a textual change from “reads ... return bits 31-0” to “reads ... return bitj 31-0,” introducing the string “bitj.”

The mcounteren.TM bit description now includes access control for stimecmp and vstimecmp: clearing TM causes less-privileged accesses to raise illegal-instruction exceptions, while setting TM permits S-mode stimecmp/vstimecmp access subject to hcounteren for VS-mode.
After changing menvcfg.ADUE, executing SFENCE.VMA with rs1=x0 and rs2=x0 is now specified to synchronize address-translation caches with the altered interpretation of PTE A/D bits.

The STCE field is no longer merely said to be furnished by the Sstc extension; the menvcfg section now directly defines STCE as the Sstc enable bit and specifies access and interrupt-pending behavior when STCE is zero.
The menvcfg section now directly defines the Zicboz CBZE field, including CBO.ZERO enable behavior and illegal-instruction behavior when disabled.
The menvcfg section now directly defines the Zicbom CBCFE field, including CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH enable behavior and illegal-instruction behavior when disabled.
The menvcfg section now directly defines the Zicbom CBIE WARL field and its encodings, including reserved encoding 10b and the behavior for encodings 00b, 01b, and 11b.

The PMM field is no longer merely said to be furnished by Smnpm; the menvcfg section now directly defines PMM behavior for Smnpm, including that PMM is read-only zero when Smnpm is not implemented or for RV32.
A table of legal PMM values was added to the menvcfg section: 00 disabled, 01 reserved, 10 PMLEN=XLEN-57, and 11 PMLEN=XLEN-48.
mseccfg changed from “an optional 64-bit read/write register” to a 64-bit read/write register that exists if any extension adding a field to mseccfg is implemented, and is otherwise reserved.
The mseccfg section now directly defines the Zkr SSEED and USEED fields controlling seed CSR access from modes less privileged than M.
An Entropy Source Access Control table was added, specifying seed CSR access behavior for M, U, S/HS, and VS/VU modes under SSEED and USEED settings.
The RLB, MMWP, and MML fields are no longer merely said to be furnished by Smepmp; the mseccfg section now directly specifies their behavior.

The mseccfg.RLB field is now directly described as a WARL field for temporarily modifying locked PMP rules, including the condition under which further modifications to RLB are ignored until PMP reset.
The mseccfg.MMWP field is now directly described as a sticky WARL bit that changes the default M-mode PMP policy for unmatched regions from ignored to denied.
The mseccfg.MML field is now directly described as a sticky WARL bit that changes interpretation of pmpcfg.L and defines M-mode-only, S/U-mode-only, and Shared-Region PMP rule behavior.

The mseccfg section now directly lists four MML rules covering changed pmpcfg.L meaning, restrictions on adding executable M-mode-only or locked Shared-Region rules, execution restrictions for M-mode code, and reservation of pmpcfg.RW=01 when MML is clear.

The PMM field is no longer merely said to be furnished by Smmpm; the mseccfg section now directly defines PMM behavior for Smmpm, including read-only-zero behavior when Smmpm is not implemented or for RV32.
A table of legal mseccfg.PMM values was added: 00 disabled, 01 reserved, 10 PMLEN=XLEN-57, and 11 PMLEN=XLEN-48.

A note was added that Smmpm implementations need to satisfy max(largest supported virtual address size, largest supported supervisor physical address size) ≤ XLEN-PMLEN to avoid masking logic on the TLB access path.
The mseccfgh existence rule was clarified to state that mseccfgh exists when XLEN=32 and mseccfg is implemented, and does not exist when XLEN=64.
Reset behavior for mseccfg.MML, mseccfg.MMWP, and mseccfg.RLB was added: they are set to 0 unless the platform mandates different reset values.
Reset behavior for mseccfg.USEED and mseccfg.SSEED was added: they must have defined reset values and must not be left undefined after reset.
AMOArithmetic was clarified to indicate support for all RISC-V AMOs defined by the A extension, rather than “all RISC-V AMOs.”
Three Zacas-related AMO PMA levels were added: AMOCASW, AMOCASD, and AMOCASQ, defining support for AMOCAS.W, AMOCAS.D, and AMOCAS.Q.
A note was added explaining that AMOCASW/D/Q require AMOArithmetic support because AMOCAS.W/D/Q require arithmetic comparison and swap operations.
The Zabha extension was added to the AMO PMA discussion, specifying that Zabha AMOs require the same level of support as corresponding A-extension or Zacas instructions.
The set of instructions to which the misaligned atomicity granule PMA applies was expanded to include compressed encodings of the listed loads and stores.
The PMP permission-fault description now includes cache-block management instructions as load-type accesses requiring read permission.
The PMP permission-fault description now includes cache-block zero instructions as store-type accesses requiring write permission.

A note was added explaining that the address 2^(XLEN+2) cannot be represented as the top of a TOR range, so TOR PMP cannot grant less-privileged access to the uppermost word in an RV32 system with 34-bit physical addresses.
The statement “If the current XLEN is greater than MXLEN, the PMP address registers are zero-extended from MXLEN to XLEN bits for the purposes of address matching” was removed.
The PMP matching logic was rewritten in terms of individual memory operations rather than accesses, clarifying behavior when one instruction decomposes into multiple memory operations.

The discussion of misaligned loads, stores, and instruction fetches being decomposed into multiple operations was moved to the beginning of the priority/matching subsection and tied explicitly to independent PMP checking of each memory operation.

The RV32 description of state-enable registers was changed from saying the listed registers are 32-bit with corresponding high-half CSRs to saying there are CSR addresses for accessing the upper 32 bits of corresponding machine-level and hypervisor CSRs.
The SRMCFG bit description was changed to reference the Ssqosid extension generally rather than “the Ssqosid Chapter 18 extension.”
The Smepmp introduction no longer names the two mechanisms as SMAP and SMEP; it describes them generically as two mechanisms that prevent OS access to and execution from unprivileged memory.

The description of existing RISC-V support was simplified from “support for SMAP, through sstatus.SUM, and for SMEP by always denying execution...” to support for the former through sstatus.SUM and the latter through denying supervisor execution of U-bit pages.
A sentence was added pointing to Appendix A.1 for the rationale that guided development of the Smepmp extension.
The threat-model text now says attacks performed on Supervisor mode are mitigated through the virtual-memory system, rather than through SMAP/SMEP.

differences.section: 6.2. Proposal / Smepmp Physical Memory Protection Rules
differences.description: The first document’s “6.2. Proposal” section, which directly introduced mseccfg and its RLB, MMWP, and MML fields, was replaced by “6.2. Smepmp Physical Memory Protection Rules,” which refers to the mseccfg section for detailed field specifications.

differences.section: 6.2. Proposal / Smepmp Physical Memory Protection Rules
differences.description: The detailed numbered proposal text defining the mseccfg CSR address, RLB field, MMWP field, and MML field was removed from Chapter 6 and relocated into the mseccfg section.
The Smepmp rationale was moved from Chapter 6 section 6.4 to a new Appendix A.1 titled “Historical Rationale for Extensions.”

The sentence listing CSR numbers 0x321, 0x322, 0x721, and 0x722 for mcyclecfg, minstretcfg, mcyclecfgh, and minstretcfgh was removed from this section; those allocations now appear in the central CSR listing.
The mnepc masking description was corrected from saying masking occurs for the implicit read by the MRET instruction to saying it occurs for the implicit read by the MNRET instruction.
The chapter title changed from “Smcdeleg Counter Delegation Extension” to “Smcdeleg/Ssccfg Counter Delegation Extensions.”
The introduction was changed from singular “This extension provides...” and “This extension also defines...” to plural “These extensions provide...” and “These extensions also defines...”.
A requirement was added that, for a RISC-V hardware platform, Smcdeleg and Ssccfg must always be implemented in tandem.

The explanatory text after the indirect HPM state mapping table was changed to specify immediately that hpmeventi and cyclecfg/instretcfg may be subsets of machine-level state, and that bit 62 MINH is read-only zero through sireg* when Sscofpmf or Smcntrpmf is implemented.
The note identifying Smaia/Ssaia CSRs now lists mvip, mvien, and hvien as defined by Smaia/Ssaia, whereas the first document listed only mvien and hvien.
The ctrtarget definition now specifies that for a not-taken branch, ctrtarget holds the PC of the next sequential instruction following the branch.
The stval description now includes hardware-error exceptions among exceptions for which a nonzero stval contains the faulting virtual address for instruction fetches, loads, or stores.
A rule was added that on a breakpoint exception raised by EBREAK or C.EBREAK, stval is written with either zero or the virtual address of the instruction.
The stval descriptions for misaligned load/store faults and instruction access/page faults now include hardware-error exceptions.
The senvcfg section now directly defines the Zicboz CBZE field and its U-mode CBO.ZERO enable behavior.
The senvcfg section now directly defines the Zicbom CBCFE field and its U-mode CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH enable behavior.
The senvcfg section now directly defines the Zicbom CBIE field, including reserved encoding 10b and the behavior of encodings 01b and 11b for U-mode CBO.INVAL.
The PMM field is no longer merely said to be furnished by Ssnpm; the senvcfg section now directly defines PMM behavior, including read-only-zero behavior when Ssnpm is not implemented or for RV32.
A table of legal senvcfg.PMM values was added: 00 disabled, 01 reserved, 10 PMLEN=XLEN-57, and 11 PMLEN=XLEN-48.
The supervisor stimecmp register specification was added as subsection 12.1.12 within the Supervisor CSRs chapter.

The stimecmp description was moved from the Sstc chapter into the Supervisor CSRs chapter, while retaining the 64-bit precision, RV32 stimecmph access, interrupt pending, and spurious interrupt behavior.

The explanation of stale translations after PTE modification was expanded: instead of saying either the old or new translation may be used, it now says any translation valid since the most recent subsuming SFENCE.VMA may be used, the choice is unpredictable, and subsequent accesses may use different translations.
The SFENCE.VMA section now states that stale translation behavior applies even if the virtual-address width for that translation differs from the width currently specified by satp.MODE.
The SFENCE.VMA section now states that for a given virtual address, any global translation since the last subsuming SFENCE.VMA may be used regardless of ASID and virtual-address width.

The SFENCE.VMA section now states that no SFENCE.VMA is required after enabling or disabling pointer masking because pointer masking affects only the effective address and not memory-management data structures.
The PTE permission fault rules now include cache-block management instructions as load-type instructions requiring read permission.
The PTE permission fault rules now include cache-block zero instructions as store-type instructions requiring write permission.
The A/D-bit update text changed “tablewalk checks” to “page-table walk checks.”
Step 9 now specifies that the PMA/PMP check is for a store to the PTE at address a+va.vpn[i]×PTESIZE, rather than simply “a store to pte.”
Step 9 now explicitly states that after setting pte.a and possibly pte.d, the updated pte is stored to the PTE at address a+va.vpn[i]×PTESIZE.
The Sv48 leaf-page-size list was changed from generic “pages, megapages, gigapages, and terapages” to explicit sizes: 4 KiB pages, 2 MiB megapages, 1 GiB gigapages, and 512 GiB terapages.

The Sv57 leaf-page-size list was changed from generic “pages, megapages, gigapages, terapages, and petapages” to explicit sizes: 4 KiB pages, 2 MiB megapages, 1 GiB gigapages, 512 GiB terapages, and 256 TiB petapages.
The Svnapot section now states that if the hypervisor extension is implemented, Svnapot is also supported in G-stage translation.
A new Svrsw60t59b extension section was added, defining bits 60–59 of PTEs as reserved for supervisor software and ignored by the implementation.
The new Svrsw60t59b section states that, when the Hypervisor extension is implemented, bits 60–59 of G-stage PTEs are also reserved for supervisor software and ignored by the implementation.
The new Svrsw60t59b section states that the extension depends on Sv39.

A rationale note was added explaining that operating systems use reserved PTE bits for metadata and that, by default, Sv39 and Sv39x4 raise page-fault or guest-page-fault exceptions if bits 60–59 are nonzero.

The Sstc chapter was condensed to an overview that references the stimecmp, vstimecmp, menvcfg.STCE, and henvcfg.STCE definitions now integrated into other sections, instead of containing detailed subsections for machine/supervisor additions, hypervisor additions, and environment configuration support.

The standalone Sstc subsection “Machine and Supervisor Level Additions” and its nested subsections were removed from the Sstc chapter; their content was integrated into Machine and Supervisor CSR sections.
The standalone Sstc subsection “Hypervisor Extension Additions” and its nested subsections were removed from the Sstc chapter; their content was integrated into the Hypervisor chapter.
The standalone Sstc environment configuration subsection was removed from the Sstc chapter; STCE behavior is now specified in the menvcfg and henvcfg sections.
The hstatus section now directly defines the HUPMM field added by Ssnpm, including its control of pointer masking for HLV.* and HSV.* instructions in U-mode when accessing as VU-mode.
A note was added advising that a hypervisor should copy the guest’s senvcfg.PMM value to hstatus.HUPMM before invoking HLV.* or HSV.* in U-mode.

differences.section: 15.2.3. Hypervisor Interrupt (hvip, hip, and hie) Registers
differences.description: The VSTIP description was changed from a logical-OR of hvip.VSTIP and any platform-specific timer signal to a logical-OR of hvip.VSTIP and, when Sstc is implemented, the timer interrupt signal from vstimecmp.

differences.section: 15.2.3. Hypervisor Interrupt (hvip, hip, and hie) Registers
differences.description: The hip.VSTIP behavior for vstimecmp was integrated into the hypervisor interrupt section, specifying when writes to vstimecmp set or clear hip.VSTIP and that hip.VSTIP remains defined for both V=0 and V=1.

The STCE field is no longer merely said to be furnished by Sstc; the henvcfg section now directly defines STCE as enabling vstimecmp for VS-mode and specifies virtual-instruction behavior when disabled.
The henvcfg section now directly defines Zicboz CBZE behavior for CBO.ZERO in VS and VU modes, including HS-qualified checks and virtual-instruction exceptions.
The henvcfg section now directly defines Zicbom CBCFE behavior for CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH in VS and VU modes, including HS-qualified checks and virtual-instruction exceptions.
The henvcfg section now directly defines Zicbom CBIE behavior for CBO.INVAL in VS and VU modes, including reserved encoding 10b, HS-qualified checks, and behavior for encodings 01b and 11b.

The PMM field is no longer merely said to be furnished by Ssnpm; the henvcfg section now directly defines PMM behavior for VS-mode pointer masking, including read-only-zero behavior when Ssnpm is not implemented or for RV32.
A table of legal henvcfg.PMM values was added: 00 disabled, 01 reserved, 10 PMLEN=XLEN-57, and 11 PMLEN=XLEN-48.
The hcounteren.TM bit description now includes access control for vstimecmp via stimecmp in VS-mode.
The virtual supervisor timer register specification was added as subsection 15.2.19 within the Hypervisor CSRs chapter.

The vstimecmp description was moved from the Sstc chapter into the Hypervisor CSRs chapter, while retaining the 64-bit precision, RV32 vstimecmph access, interrupt pending, and SEE compatibility text.

differences.section: 15.2.18. Virtual Supervisor Address Translation and Protection (vsatp) Register
differences.description: The note about HLV, HLVX, or HSV instructions not setting VS-stage A bits changed from “misspeculatively executed” to “mispredicted.”

differences.section: 15.3.1. Hypervisor Virtual-Machine Load and Store Instructions
differences.description: The hypervisor load/store instruction section now states that memory accesses performed by HLVX.* instructions are not subject to pointer masking.

differences.section: 15.3.1. Hypervisor Virtual-Machine Load and Store Instructions
differences.description: A note was added explaining that HLVX.* instructions emulate implicit instruction-fetch accesses from guest memory and are exempt from pointer masking for that reason; the same rationale is given for pointer masking not applying when MXR is set.

The G bit in G-stage PTEs changed from being described as “reserved for future standard use” to being “currently not used,” while retaining the requirement that software clear it until defined and hardware ignore it.
The synchronization requirement was expanded from changes to menvcfg.PBMTE only to changes to menvcfg.PBMTE or menvcfg.ADUE.
The synchronization requirement was expanded from changes to henvcfg.PBMTE only to changes to henvcfg.PBMTE or henvcfg.ADUE.
The world-switch note was expanded from henvcfg.PBMTE to henvcfg.PBMTE/ADUE.
A new Hypervisor subsection “Interaction with Pointer Masking” was added, moving the GPA-width and HFENCE.GVMA pointer-masking rules into the Hypervisor chapter.

The new subsection states that with vsatp.MODE=Bare, the two extra GPA bits may be subject to pointer masking depending on hgatp.MODE and senvcfg.PMM/henvcfg.PMM, while this issue does not apply when vsatp.MODE is not Bare.

The new subsection states that hypervisors should execute HFENCE.GVMA with rs1=x0 when henvcfg.PMM changes to or from values where XLEN-PMLEN is less than the GPA width for sv57x4 with PMLEN 7 or 16, or sv48x4 with PMLEN 16.
The new subsection states that address-specific HFENCE.GVMA should either ignore the address argument or ignore top masked GPA bits when comparing for an address match.
In the hypervisor trap-cause table, interrupt exception code 13 changed from “Reserved for counter-overflow interrupt” to “Counter-overflow interrupt.”

differences.section: 15.6.3. Transformed Instruction or Pseudoinstruction for mtinst or htinst
differences.description: Figure captions for transformed load and store instructions were shortened by removing the word “noncompressed,” although the preceding text still says the instructions are not compressed.

differences.section: 16.1.2. Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps
differences.description: The text was corrected from “an software-check exception caused by the LPAD instruction itself (See [LP_INST])” to “a software-check exception caused by the LPAD instruction itself,” removing the [LP_INST] reference.

The Bare-mode shadow stack rule was reworded from “shadow stack memory accesses are prohibited, and shadow stack instructions will raise...” to “shadow stack instructions raise...” for effective privilege modes less than M.
The M-mode SSAMOSWAP rule was reworded from “any memory access by an SSAMOSWAP.W/D instruction will result...” to “memory access by an SSAMOSWAP.W/D instruction results...”.
The note describing why access-fault exceptions are used changed “triggering an access-fault” to “triggering an access fault.”
The list of privilege modes in which tools can be applied was punctuated as “U, S, and M” instead of “U, S and M.”

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: C.LQSP was removed from the compressed-instruction list of explicit memory accesses subject to pointer masking.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: C.SQSP was removed from the compressed-instruction list of explicit memory accesses subject to pointer masking.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The Hypervisor Extension bullet now refers to HLV.* and HSV.* pointer-masking behavior via the hstatus section instead of via the former Ssnpm ISA-extension subsection.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The description of hardware writes to CSRs was qualified to say pointer masking is applied “when applicable” rather than unconditionally.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The description of debug address-trigger matching was qualified to say pointer masking is applied “when applicable.”

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The stval example was changed to say hardware applies pointer masking to an address written into stval if pointer masking is applicable to that address, rather than saying pointer masking is applied to any address written into stval.
The Ssnpm bullet now includes references to the senvcfg, henvcfg, hstatus, and hypervisor pointer-masking interaction sections.
The Smnpm bullet now references the menvcfg section for its definition.
The Smmpm bullet now references the mseccfg section for its definition.
The standalone “18.3. ISA Extensions” section from the first document was removed; its PMM field definitions were integrated into the relevant CSR sections.
The standalone PMM value table in the Pointer Masking chapter was removed; PMM value tables now appear in the menvcfg, mseccfg, senvcfg, and henvcfg sections.

The standalone Ssnpm subsection defining senvcfg.PMM, henvcfg.PMM, hstatus.HUPMM, and HLVX pointer-masking behavior was removed from the Pointer Masking chapter and integrated into the supervisor and hypervisor CSR sections.
The standalone Smnpm subsection defining menvcfg.PMM was removed from the Pointer Masking chapter and integrated into the menvcfg section.
The standalone Smmpm subsection defining mseccfg.PMM was removed from the Pointer Masking chapter and integrated into the mseccfg section.

The standalone pointer-masking subsection stating that no SFENCE.VMA is required after enabling or disabling pointer masking was removed from the Pointer Masking chapter; the rule now appears in the SFENCE.VMA section.

The standalone pointer-masking subsection on two-stage address translation was removed from the Pointer Masking chapter; its content now appears in the Hypervisor chapter as “Interaction with Pointer Masking.”
The “Number of Masked Bits” content was moved from subsection 24.3.6 under ISA Extensions to subsection 18.2.8 under Background.
The privileged instruction listing now includes a “Control Transfer Records Management Instructions” category.
The SCTRCLR instruction encoding was added to the privileged instruction listing: funct7/funct12 pattern 0001000 00100 with rs1=00000, funct3=000, rd=00000, opcode=1110011.
The History chapter was renumbered from Chapter 26 to Chapter 20.
The standalone Bibliography page and heading were removed; the bibliography entries now appear at the end of Appendix A.1 text in the parsed second document.

differences.section: Appendix A: Historical Rationale for Extensions
differences.description: A new Appendix A was added to contain historical rationale for RISC-V ISA extensions in chronological order.

differences.section: Appendix A: Historical Rationale for Extensions
differences.description: Appendix A explains that rationales for extensions ratified before the appendix was conceived will be added over time or synthesized from the historical record if not recorded.

differences.section: A.1. “Smepmp” Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode
differences.description: The Smepmp rationale text formerly in Chapter 6 was added as Appendix A.1.
